braincell_check.q: In 'Sex and the City', what is the name of the columnist main character?
What year did the first Playboy magazine publish?
Which of these is NOT a real cocktail?
In the movie 'Grease', what is the name of the risqué group Sandy joins?
What is the bestselling erotic novel series of all time?
Which ancient Roman holiday involved lots of debauchery and role reversal between social classes?
What does the French phrase 'joie de vivre' literally translate to?
In the TV show 'Friends', how many times did Ross get divorced?
Which city is famously known as 'the city that never sleeps' and has the reputation for its nightlife?
What is the name of the famous burlesque nightclub in Paris?
In psychology, 'the forbidden fruit effect' refers to what phenomenon?
braincell_check.a: Wanting things more when they're restricted
options: Wanting things more when they're restricted

braincell_check.q: Which Jane Austen novel features a passionate love story often described as more intense than her others?
What is 'The Notebook' actor Ryan Gosling's real middle name?
Which of these is a real scent classification in perfumery?
What percentage of people, according to a 2023 study, have matched with someone they already know on a dating app?
Which Shakespeare play features one of the most famous romantic balcony scenes in literary history?
What is the world's most expensive perfume ingredient, sourced from sperm whales?
Which decade saw the invention of the waterbed?
In Rihanna's famous lyric, she sings 'come here, rude boy, boy can you get it up?' — what album is this from?
What is the term for the phenomenon where people find strangers more attractive after a scary experience together?
Which city is considered the honeymoon capital of North America?
What does 'DTR' mean in modern dating slang?
Which musician said 'I'd rather have a bottle in front of me than a frontal lobotomy'?
What is the name of the red light district in Amsterdam?
In the original Dirty Dancing movie, what is the name of Baby's love interest?
What psychological principle explains why playing hard to get sometimes increases attraction?
What is the Italian word for 'sweet doing nothing' — the pleasure of doing absolutely nothing?
How many positions are illustrated in the original Kama Sutra text?
What is the name of Beyoncé's album that was considered her most intimate and personal confession?
In which country did the tradition of 'bundling boards' exist — where courting couples would share a bed with a board between them?

truth_cache: What is the most forward thing you've ever done to get someone's attention, and did it actually work?
Describe your ideal flirtatious text exchange — what opener would actually get you to respond every time?
Have you ever had feelings for someone you absolutely weren't supposed to? You don't have to name names, but describe who.
What's the most romantic thing someone has ever done for you that you've never told anyone about?
When were you last genuinely jealous because of someone in this room or someone connected to this room? Be honest.
What's the most flattering lie someone has told you that you absolutely knew was a lie but chose to believe anyway?
Describe the most unexpectedly attractive quality you've noticed in a stranger recently — and be specific.
What's something you do to feel attractive that you've never admitted to anyone?
Have you ever caught yourself composing a message you never sent? What did it say and who was it for?
What's the most dramatic thing you've done after a breakup that you're either proud or ashamed of?
What is something a past partner did that you wish a current person in your life would do?
Have you ever had a 'situationship' that you still haven't fully processed? Describe without names.
What's your most embarrassing story involving attraction, a moment you wanted to be smooth and spectacularly weren't?
Describe the most uncomfortable crush you've ever had — someone you really shouldn't have been into.
What's something you find physically attractive in people that you've never admitted out loud?
Tell us about a time you did something completely out of character to impress someone romantically. Did it work?
Have you ever misread a signal so badly that you cringe every time you think about it? Tell the story.
What is the most romantic moment of your life to date? Be embarrassingly detailed.
What's a relationship red flag that you absolutely ignored until it became a full emergency?
Have you ever kept dating someone longer than you should have because the physical chemistry was too good to quit?
What's the most transparent excuse you've ever used to spend more time with someone you were interested in?
Describe your most chaotic 'almost' — the relationship that nearly happened and still makes you wonder 'what if'.
What's something you've said in the heat of the moment that surprised even yourself?
What do you consider your most attractive quality that you think most people in this room haven't noticed yet?
Have you ever been the reason for a 'we need to talk' conversation? What happened?
What's the longest you've maintained a flirtatious tension with someone without acting on it, and how did it resolve?
Describe a moment when someone's confidence completely won you over — what were they doing?
What's a bold thing you've wanted to do or say to someone in this room tonight but haven't acted on?
When was the last time you were genuinely starstruck — not by a celebrity but by someone you actually know?
What compliment would make you absolutely melt right now, and who in this room is most likely to mean it if they said it?
glitch_dare: Lock eyes with the person to your right without blinking or looking away for 30 full seconds. Maintain a completely neutral expression.
Write a genuinely flirtatious compliment about someone in the room on a piece of paper, seal it, and hand it to them. They open it later.
Demonstrate your best 'I'm interested in you' smile at whoever the group points at. Hold it for 10 seconds.
Slow dance by yourself to whatever song is currently playing, as if you're at the most romantic moment of your life.
Whisper something genuinely sweet to the person on your left — loud enough that only they can hear. They can choose whether to share.
Do your most convincing impression of someone passionately in love — recite a dramatic declaration to a houseplant.
Describe, in uncomfortably specific detail, your ideal date night from start to finish. You have 60 seconds.
Initiate a completely unannounced and enthusiastic group hug. You have 5 seconds to convince everyone to participate.
Give the person across from you three genuine, specific compliments — none of them can be about physical appearance.
Let one player choose a romantic movie scene — you act out the lead character's role with a pillow as your co-star.
Put on a song and lip-sync to it directed entirely at one person in the room. They have to maintain eye contact.
Write a three-line flirtatious poem about the person who is sitting the farthest away from you. Read it aloud.
Do a 45-second impression of someone deeply, overwhelmingly in love. Pick your method — speech, movement, expression.
Propose a fictional toast that is sincerely romantic but aimed at the group generally. Make people feel something.
Hold hands with whoever is sitting next to you for the next full round. No letting go unless a game event forces it.
Describe in two sentences why the person to your left is genuinely, objectively attractive. Be specific and sincere.
Give someone in the room a shoulder massage for 30 seconds. They pick who does it — you just have to accept.
Tell the group about a moment you felt genuinely desired — be vague enough to keep your dignity, specific enough to be interesting.
Do a 60-second confident walk around the room making meaningful eye contact with every single person.
Send a voice message to someone not at this party saying 'I miss you more than is probably healthy.' Read it aloud before sending.
Choose someone in the room and describe them using only food metaphors for 30 seconds straight.
Reenact the most romantic scene from any movie using only props available in the room right now.
Give a sincere, detailed answer to: 'What would a perfect morning after a perfect date with you look like?'
Tell the person to your right one thing about them that you noticed tonight that you didn't notice before.
Attempt to teach the group a slow dance move. Everyone must try it. The worst dancer gets to lead next round.
Describe your 'type' in embarrassing, specific detail for 60 seconds. Hold nothing back.
Send a completely genuine 'you looked really good the last time I saw you' text to someone outside this party. Read their reply out loud.
Do the most dramatic 'running toward someone in slow motion' scene across the room. Pick your imaginary target and commit fully.
Tell the group the most genuinely romantic song lyric you know and explain why it hits you so hard.
Describe a completely fictional but deeply romantic meet-cute story set in this exact room tonight. Make it believable.
prompt_duel: Flirt-off: both players have 45 seconds to flirt with a houseplant as convincingly as possible. Group votes who's more convincing.
Write the most persuasive 'please respond to my text' follow-up message. Group picks the best one.
Both players deliver a 30-second confessional about the other player as if narrating a dating show. Group picks the spicier narration.
Eye contact challenge: both players stare at each other for 30 seconds while the group narrates a dramatic scene around them.
Both improvise a 'we just met at a party' opener to the same third person in the room. Group votes on who gets the number.
Speed compliment battle: players alternate compliments. First to repeat a compliment type or go silent for 3 seconds loses.
Both write opening lines for a dating profile about the other player. The subject says which one they'd swipe right on.
Charisma test: both players have 30 seconds to convince the group they're 'the interesting one' at a dinner party.
Both players confess their most transparent 'I was clearly into you' behavior from a past situation. Group votes whose was more obvious.
Describe the same person (someone famous, agreed on in advance) as both terrifyingly attractive and deeply annoying. Who makes the better case?
Both players have 60 seconds to plan the most impressive first date on zero dollars. Group votes on the better date.
Red flag or green flag: both players defend the same controversial relationship behavior from opposite sides. Group votes on who's right.
Both players must convince the group they have better taste in music for someone's romantic playlist. Each names 3 songs — group votes.
Describe your ideal partner's morning routine — but make it sound like a dating app prompt. More appealing description wins.
Both players do a 30-second impression of the other player trying to flirt. The subject rates their own impression.
Confessional speed round: who can list more small romantic mishaps in 45 seconds?
Both explain 'what makes a great kisser' without being explicit. Most convincing explanation wins.
Both players write a 'missed connection' post about someone in the room without naming them. Group guesses who it's about.
Describe your most charismatic quality and least charismatic quality simultaneously. Who gives the more self-aware answer?
Both players tell the same story — a romantic success — from their own perspective. Group votes on whose version sounds better.
Comfort level challenge: both reveal something mildly personal they've never told the group. Whoever goes more vulnerable wins.
Both players pitch a movie about a couple who met at this exact party tonight. Most compelling pitch wins.
Both describe what they consider the single most attractive non-physical quality in a partner. Group debates who has higher standards.
Both players do a dramatic reading of the same pre-written cliché love letter with completely opposite emotional deliveries.
Most awkward first date story: whoever's story gets the bigger collective wince wins.
Both players have 30 seconds to describe the plot of a romance novel they just made up. Group picks which one they'd actually read.
Who has the better answer to: 'What's the most underrated quality in a romantic partner?'
Both write a text they wish they'd sent in a past situation but didn't. Group decides whose situation was more interesting.
Both players describe the perfect apology after forgetting a significant date. Group votes on who's more convincing.
Describe yourself in three words as if you were writing a dating profile, then describe the other player in three words. Compare.
robot_slapdown: Romantic override: you must speak exclusively in compliments for the next 2 full rounds — not just to one person, to everyone.
The AI has flagged excessive guardedness. Tell the group one thing about yourself that you rarely share at parties.
Seduction mode activated by mistake: you must dramatically wink at the next person who speaks to you, whatever the context.
System diagnosis: low vulnerability detected. Share something that genuinely made you smile this week, with full context.
Chaos flirt protocol: the group writes a flirtatious dare on your behalf and you must execute it within 2 minutes.
Embarrassment subroutine: describe, in slow motion narration, the most recent time you were flustered by someone.
Attention scan complete: you must give the last person who walked into the room a genuine 'you looked great tonight' compliment.
Overconfidence detected: you must ask someone in the room a genuine question you've been too polite to ask all night.
Romantic memory cache: share the single most swoon-worthy thing anyone has ever said or done for you. Spare no detail.
Glitch in the matrix: for the next round, you must describe everything you're experiencing as if narrating a romance novel.
Proximity error: you must sit directly next to whoever you've sat furthest from all night for the next 3 rounds.
Charisma drain detected: the group must collectively give you a pep talk before you can take your next turn.
Attraction algorithm misfired: confess which person outside this party you'd invite if you could add one more person tonight.
Mystery protocol: you may only respond to questions with questions for the next full round.
Pulse rate spike detected: describe in detail the last moment something made your heart actually race — any context counts.
Affection bandwidth exceeded: everyone you physically touch for the next round must receive a compliment within 3 seconds.
Nostalgia subroutine: share the most romantic song that always takes you back to a specific moment in your past.
Chemistry scan active: describe the specific combination of qualities you find most irresistibly attractive in a person.
Tension protocol: maintain unbroken eye contact with whoever is sitting diagonally from you every time you speak for the next round.
Vulnerability upload complete: share the last time you felt genuinely nervous around someone in the best possible way.
system_crash: After dark protocol: the lights go off (or get dimmed) for exactly 60 seconds while someone reads a dramatic monologue from any romantic movie they know.
Everyone simultaneously admits one thing they find genuinely attractive about the person on their right — no filtering allowed.
Mass vote: who in this room has the most magnetic personality tonight? The winner must share their secret for at least 60 seconds.
Chaos confession round: everyone writes one mild secret about their romantic life on a paper — mix them up, read them anonymously.
Group conspiracy: everyone must pretend the person in the center of the room is the main character of a romance movie. Each person narrates one sentence of their story.
All phones out: everyone shares the last emoji they sent in a romantic or close-friendship context and explains the full situation.
The big question: everyone answers 'what's the most romantic city you've ever visited or want to visit, and why' — 20 seconds each.
Synchronized vulnerability: everyone must stand up and state one thing they genuinely want right now — no game-related answers allowed.
Who in this room would be most likely to be pined after from afar? Group votes. The winner explains how they'd never even notice.
Lights-out challenge: everyone describes their ideal romantic atmosphere in exactly 5 words. Group votes on the most evocative description.
After midnight rule: for the next 5 minutes, everyone must speak 10% more slowly and make at least one second of eye contact with whoever they address.
Confession carousel: go around the room — everyone completes the sentence 'The most romantic thing about me is...' without irony.
Playlist takeover: the person with the most romantic music taste (group votes) controls the next 10 minutes of music. No skips allowed.
Synchronized truth: everyone must simultaneously say the name of the first person who made them feel butterflies. On three. Ready?
Group heatmap: everyone points at whoever they think is giving off the strongest 'good night energy' right now. That person gives a toast.
Double-blind compliment round: everyone writes a compliment about someone in the room, folds it, and places it in a pile. Each person draws one and reads it as if it's about them.
Memory lane: go around the room — everyone shares a 10-second version of their most cinematic real-life romantic moment.
Ambient drama: the group must collectively decide on the 'ships' in this room tonight (who's giving what energy). No one is exempted from the analysis.
After dark freeze: everyone freezes in place for 20 seconds. The person who moves first must answer whatever question the group invents.
Vibe check: everyone rates the current party atmosphere from 1-10 on one specific romantic metric they name. Discuss the results.
crowd_override: Group vote: who in this room would be most irresistible in a black-and-white 1950s movie? They must strike the pose.
The group collectively plans a 'perfect evening out' for two players they think should experience it together. They present the plan.
Everyone anonymously votes for who they think is secretly the most romantic person in the room. Reveal and debate.
Group decision: what era of dating would each person in the room thrive in? Everyone gets assigned their era and must argue for it.
Crowd rates everyone's 'walking into a room' energy from 1-10 based on how they entered tonight. Be honest.
Everyone in the group assigns each player their 'rom-com archetype' (the hopeless romantic, the commitment-phobe, the mysterious stranger, etc.).
Group decides: which two people in this room have the most undeniable chemistry as friends? They must take a photo together right now.
Vote: who in the room has the most interesting romantic history based purely on vibes? They share one chapter of it.
Crowd picks the most compelling opening line from tonight's game and decides who should actually use it this week on a real person.
Group assigns everyone a 'signature move' they must use once before the game ends — based on the group's read of their personality.
Everyone writes 'the vibe you're giving off tonight' about each player anonymously. Compile and read each player's results.
Group votes on who would survive longest on a romantic reality show like Love Island or The Bachelor. They must pitch themselves.
Crowd pick: everyone simultaneously points to who they'd want as their wingperson for a night out. Most selected person gives advice.
The group designs the perfect partner for each player in under 3 minutes. Each person hears their custom match profile.
Vote: whose style would the group most like to steal for a night? That person gives a 60-second styling consultation.
Group collaboratively writes a 5-sentence love story set at this exact party tonight using real first names. Read it aloud.
Everyone votes on who has the best advice for navigating awkward romantic situations. That person solves a scenario the group invents.
Group assigns each player a 'dating show' they'd win — The Bachelor, Love Is Blind, Too Hot to Handle, etc. Each player argues.
Crowd override: the group collectively rewrites the rules of one current social media dating behavior. Everyone must agree on the new rule.
Final crowd verdict: who had the best overall energy tonight? That person gives a 30-second closing speech about the evening.
holo_drama: It's the season finale of your romantic reality show. Describe what the last episode has been building toward and deliver your final rose ceremony speech.
You're the mysterious new arrival at a glamorous party who everyone's been warned about. Introduce yourself as that character for the next 5 minutes.
The chemistry between you and someone in this room has finally reached a boiling point according to the show producers. Narrate the moment.
You're delivering the most iconic 'I almost let you go' speech in movie history. Deliver it. To whoever the group chooses.
You play the romantic lead who just realized the person they've been looking for has been in the room all along. React accordingly.
Describe the scene from the movie of your life where the soundtrack swells and everything clicks into place.
You are the romantic rival who is devastatingly charming and completely, infuriatingly likable. Introduce yourself.
The group has just voted that your energy tonight is 'the love interest.' Lean into it for 2 full minutes.
Narrate yourself walking into your own love story for the first time. Give us the internal monologue, the details, the whole scene.
You're on the last night of a trip where you met someone unforgettable. Describe the conversation happening on this fictional rooftop.
Deliver the apology speech your character owes someone — real or fictional. Make it sincere, make it count.
You're the best friend in a rom-com who gives the protagonist the speech that changes everything. Who are they? What do you say?
Your character just got the text they've been waiting three months for. React. In full, without explaining.
Describe in cinematic detail the first moment your character genuinely realized they were falling for someone — anyone, real or fictional.
You're the charming stranger at a bar who says exactly the right thing and then disappears. What do you say in your one scene?
It's the mid-movie crisis. Your character has made a terrible decision in the name of self-protection. Monologue your way through it.
You've just discovered you've been the villain in someone else's love story. Have the self-aware reckoning out loud.
The climactic airport scene. You're running. Describe who you're running toward, why, and what you say when you get there.
You are the slow burn. Describe in detail what it looks like when your character finally stops holding back.
It's the epilogue. Five years later. Describe where your character ended up and who, if anyone, they're with.
firewall_bonus: Desire card: you may direct the next truth question at any player of your choosing — and they must answer it honestly.
Charm immunity: the next time the group votes against you, your charm automatically flips one vote in your favor.
Whisper privilege: you receive one whispered secret from the person of your choosing — they must tell you something they haven't told the group.
Veto power: play this to cancel any dare or truth directed at you and substitute one of your own invention.
Plot armor activated: skip the next punishment or consequence completely, no questions asked.
Wildcard: invent a new category not in the game and challenge one player to complete it — rules must be fair.
Rewrite history: change your answer to any previous question tonight retroactively. The new answer stands.
Audience bonus: the entire group owes you exactly one genuine, specific compliment before the next round begins.
Double take: any truth or dare you complete in the next round earns you double consideration for bonus events.
Safe passage: if someone sends a dare your way this round, you may redirect exactly half of it back at them.
Inner circle: for the next 3 rounds, you are immune to crowd votes — only direct challenges can affect you.
Story time: the group must listen in complete respectful silence while you share a genuine memory of your choice — two minutes, no interruptions.
Golden answer: your next truth response is accepted without any follow-up questions, regardless of how vague you are.
Pivot play: you may swap your current dare mid-execution if you announce 'actually, I choose...' and complete something harder.
Mood setter: you control the music for the next 5 full minutes. Total authority. No vetoes.
Shield generator: you may protect one other player from the next negative event in the game.
Time extension: you have twice as long to complete any challenge for the rest of this round.
Oracle mode: the group must ask you for advice on any relationship or social scenario they name — your answer is binding for the evening.
Full pardon: any previous embarrassing moment from tonight is officially forgiven and may not be referenced again.
Encore reward: you completed something so well the group demands a repeat — but this time you choose the format entirely.
